macro_segments.title: The Interpretation of 'Oath to a Minor' (Katan): From Heir Claims to Collection Cases

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Statement: No Oath on Claims by Deaf-Mute, Mentally Incompetent, or Minors, But Oath to a Minor
Rabbi Elazar ben Yaakov's Interpretation: The Minor as Heir, Modeh BeKtszat, and Migo Exemption
Shmuel's Reading: Oath to Minor Means Collection from Minor's Estate, Not Response to Minor's Claim
Collection from Heirs and Orphans: The Obligation of Oath Extends to Adult Heirs (Abaye's Chiddush)
Collection from Sanctified Property (Hekdesh): Scheming Concerns and the Distinction Between Deathbed Sanctification and Healthy Persons
Collection from Encumbered Property (Meshubadim): The Fear of Conspiracy to Defraud Third Parties

macro_segments.title: Objects Excluded from Oath, Double Payment, and Guardianship Obligations

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Five Categories Excluded from Oath (Slaves, Writs, Land, Sanctified Objects) and Their Implications for Other Obligations
Rabbi Shimon's Position: Kodashim with Replacement Obligations Are Treated as Property (Davar Ha-Gorem Le-Mamon), Not Sanctified Objects
Rabbi Meir vs. Sages on Laden Vines: Attachment to Land vs. Readiness for Harvest as the Criterion for Karka Status

macro_segments.title: The Requirement of Quantifiability in Modeh BeKtszat: When Claims and Admissions Must Be Expressible in Measure, Weight, or Number

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Modeh BeKtszat Applies Only to Quantifiable Claims (Measure, Weight, Number)
House Full of Potatoes: Generic 'Full' vs. Specific Reference (This House Full) and Abaye's Interpretation

macro_segments.title: Objects Excluded from Oath, Double Payment, and Guardianship Obligations (continued)

micro_segments.title: Deriving Exclusions from the Verse: General-Particular-General (Kelal-Prat-Kelal) Structure Applied to Movable, Intrinsic Value Objects
Double Payment (Kefel) Exemption: Why Land, Slaves, Writs, and Hekdesh Are Excluded
Unpaid Guardian (Shomer Chinam) and Paid Guardian (Shomer Sachar): No Oath and No Payment Obligations on Excluded Objects

macro_segments.title: The Requirement of Quantifiability in Modeh BeKtszat: When Claims and Admissions Must Be Expressible in Measure, Weight, or Number (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rava's Stricter Standard: Both Claim and Admission Must Be Articulated in Quantifiable Terms, Not Merely Quantifiable in Difference
Baraita Examples: Grain Gallons, Large vs. Small Menorot, and Belts—Distinction Between Different Objects and Partial Quantities
Principle: Both Claim and Response Must Be Quantifiable to Establish Modeh BeKtszat Oath
Menorah of Components (Chuliyot): Converting a Ten-Pound Menorah to Five-Pound by Scraping as Subset Relationship

macro_segments.title: Collateral (Mashkon) Cases: Valuation Disputes and Who Takes the Oath When Collateral Value Affects the Debt Remaining

micro_segments.title: Scenario Setup: Lender Holds Collateral Worth Less Than Loan; Dispute Over Collateral's Value and Remaining Debt
Case One: Lender Claims Collateral Worth Half Loan (50 vs. 100); Debtor Fully Denies—Borrower is Kofer HaKol, Exempt from Oath
Case Two: Lender Claims Collateral Worth 50; Debtor Admits Worth 75, Only Owes 25—Modeh BeKtszat, Debtor Takes Oath
Case Three: Debtor Claims Collateral Worth 200, Lender Owes 100; Lender Denies—Kofer HaKol, Exempt
Case Four: Debtor Claims Collateral Worth 200, Lender Owes 100; Lender Admits Only 75, Owes 25—Modeh BeKtszat, Lender Takes Oath
Mishna's Ruling: When Lender Claims 50 and Debtor Claims 75 (Thus Would Owe Only 25), the Lender Takes the Oath, Not the Debtor

macro_segments.title: Protection Against False Oath in Mashkon Case: Why the Lender Takes the Oath and the Remedy of Rav Ashi

micro_segments.title: The Concern: If Debtor Swears Collateral Was Worth More, Lender Might Later Produce It and Prove False Oath (Though Made in Good Faith)
The Solution: Lender Takes Oath Instead, Protects Debtor from Being Shown as False Swearer, But Lender Collects Full Amount
Rav Ashi's Principle: Even When Guardian Admits Liability, He Must Swear Object Is No Longer in His Possession (Shevuat Shomer)
Application of Rav Ashi: Lender Swears First (Object Not in Possession), Then Debtor Swears to Collateral Value, Protecting Debtor's Oath
Resolution: Both Parties Take Oaths in Sequence; Debtor's Oath Is Protected Because Lender Has Already Sworn to Non-Possession

topical_tags.term: Modeh BeKtszat (Admitting to Part of Claim)
